title: Postpartum Hemorrhage from Uterine Atony: Rapid Team Response
subtitle: One hour after a vaginal birth of a large baby following a long, oxytocin-augmented labor, a 29-year-old G3P3 is saturating pads with a soft, boggy uterus. The nurse must recognize postpartum hemorrhage, act on the 4 T's, and lead the team response.

patient.name: Tasha Greene
patient.admitDate: Postpartum unit, ~1 hour after a vaginal delivery of a 4.2 kg infant following prolonged labor with oxytocin augmentation. Now saturating perineal pads with a soft, boggy, displaced fundus and a rising pulse. Hemorrhage protocol activated.

vitals.temp: Pulse rising, BP falling — early shock

labs.r: Avoid Methergine in HTN, carboprost in asthma.

nursesNote: Tasha Greene, 29 (G3P3), ~1 hour after a vaginal birth of a 4.2 kg infant following prolonged oxytocin-augmented labor — all atony risk factors. Saturating pads, soft/boggy fundus displaced by a full bladder, EBL ~1200 mL, pulse rising and BP trending down. Hemorrhage protocol activated. Priorities: firm fundal massage, empty the bladder, oxytocin and ordered uterotonics (check contraindications), fluids/blood, quantify loss, and lead the team with closed-loop communication.
providerOrders: Activate the obstetric hemorrhage protocol; call for help
Firm fundal massage; ensure the bladder is empty (straight cath/Foley)
Two large-bore IVs, isotonic fluids; type and crossmatch; CBC and coags
Uterotonics: oxytocin first-line; methylergonovine (avoid if hypertensive); carboprost (avoid if asthma); misoprostol; tranexamic acid per orders
Quantify blood loss; transfuse as ordered; prepare for bimanual compression/OR if uncontrolled

opts.text: A soft, boggy uterus suggests atony — the leading cause of early PPH
Saturating a pad in ≤15 minutes or a steady trickle of blood is abnormal
Tachycardia often appears BEFORE the blood pressure drops
A full bladder can displace the uterus and prevent it from contracting
Heavy postpartum bleeding is normal and needs no intervention
Prolonged labor, a large baby, and oxytocin augmentation raise the risk
Atony (TONE) is the most common cause — assess fundal tone first
The 4 T's are Tone, Trauma, Tissue, and Thrombin
Firm fundal massage and emptying the bladder are immediate measures
Oxytocin is first-line; add other uterotonics per orders
Methylergonovine (Methergine) is safe to give in severe hypertension
Call for help early and activate the hemorrhage protocol

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the management of the hemorrhage

opts.text: Massage the fundus and empty the bladder
Call for help and activate the hemorrhage protocol
Ensure large-bore IV access, fluids, and ordered uterotonics
Quantify blood loss, monitor vitals, and give blood products as ordered
Prepare for escalation (bimanual compression/OR) and document

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Which of the 4 T's does each finding point to?

opts.text: Soft, boggy uterus that firms up with massage
Fundus boggy and displaced upward by a full bladder
Firm uterus but steady bright-red bleeding from a vaginal/cervical tear
A swollen, painful, bluish bulge in the perineum
Bleeding continues despite a firm, well-contracted, empty uterus
Placenta delivered incomplete with fragments retained
Missing cotyledons/membranes on placental inspection

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Sequence the bedside and team actions
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stay with the patient and massage the fundus while calling for help
Assign clear roles with closed-loop communication (meds, IV/labs, recorder)
Give ordered uterotonics after checking contraindications
Give fluids/blood and continuously monitor the response
Escalate to the provider/OR if bleeding continues; SBAR handoff
The uterus firms and bleeding slows after massage and oxytocin
Vital signs stabilize and the pad count decreases
Bladder emptied; fundus firm and midline
Hemoglobin stabilizes after fluids and blood products
The fundus stays boggy and bleeding continues despite massage
Pulse keeps rising as blood pressure falls — hypovolemic shock
Oozing from IV sites with abnormal clotting
Methergine is given to a hypertensive patient and her BP spikes

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Management of Care, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
